name: Cherubim
description: Cherubim (singular: cherub) are celestial beings frequently depicted in Abrahamic religions, particularly Judaism, Christianity, and Islam. Their role is multifaceted, often serving as guardians of sacred spaces and divine attendants. In the Hebrew Bible, they are most prominently associated with the Ark of the Covenant, where golden cherubim with outstretched wings overshadowed the mercy seat. Ezekiel's vision provides a more complex and symbolic depiction, portraying them as composite creatures with four faces (human, lion, ox, and eagle) and multiple wings, embodying divine attributes such as wisdom, strength, and swiftness. Christian theology further associates cherubim with the second highest order of angels in the celestial hierarchy, emphasizing their role in praising and glorifying God. While their precise function and nature remain subject to theological debate, cherubim consistently represent divine power, holiness, and the accessibility of the divine presence, whilst also serving as protectors against impurity. Their imagery is integral to religious art and architecture, signifying the sacredness of the space they inhabit and the power they represent.
subtitle: Guardians of the Divine

keyMyths.title: Guardians of the Garden of Eden
keyMyths.description: After Adam and Eve were expelled from the Garden of Eden, God placed cherubim with a flaming sword to guard the way to the tree of life. This myth highlights the cherubim's role as protectors of sacred spaces and divine decrees, preventing access to immortality for those deemed unworthy. The flaming sword symbolizes divine judgment and the consequences of disobedience, reinforcing the cherubim's role as enforcers of divine law.

keyMyths.title: Cherubim and the Ark of the Covenant
keyMyths.description: The Ark of the Covenant, housed within the Tabernacle and later the Temple in Jerusalem, featured two golden cherubim with outstretched wings overshadowing the mercy seat. This imagery symbolized God's presence dwelling among his people and the accessibility of divine grace. The cherubim served as intermediaries, representing the connection between the earthly and the divine realms, emphasizing the sacredness of the Ark and its contents.

keyMyths.title: Ezekiel's Vision of the Cherubim
keyMyths.description: Ezekiel's vision depicts cherubim as complex, composite creatures with four faces (human, lion, ox, and eagle) and multiple wings, each representing different aspects of divine power and attributes. These creatures were associated with the throne of God, symbolizing his sovereignty and omnipresence. Their movements were swift and coordinated, demonstrating divine efficiency and control over the cosmos, solidifying their role as agents of divine will.
